label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this question. The question is asking why the writer tells visitors and drivers to look around when traveling. The options are A through D. Looking at the passage, the last paragraph says, "The next time you visit a park or drive through an area with a lot of wildlife, look around. You might see an animal overpass!" So the key part here is "You might see an animal overpass!" which directly relates to the ecopassages mentioned earlier in the article. The ecopassages are the bridges and underpasses that animals use to cross roads safely. The article discusses how these structures help animals avoid road accidents and even provides examples of animals using them, like mountain lions leaving footprints. The purpose of mentioning looking around is to point out that these ecopassages exist and that animals are using them, so you might spot them. Looking at the answer choices: (A) Wild animals may attack cars – The article doesn't mention animals attacking cars. It's about animals crossing roads safely, not attacking. (B) Wild animals may jam the road – Jamming the road isn't discussed either. The issue is roadkill, not traffic jams caused by animals. (C) They may see wild animals in the park – While the article mentions wildlife in parks, the specific reason for looking around is tied to ecopassages, not just seeing animals in general in parks. (D) They may see wild animals on ecopassages
